$comment: Hand-curated Enneagram distribution data from primary sources. Source of truth: docs/research/enneagram-public-distributions.md. Review before each deploy that changes the comparison section. Not auto-generated.

sources.name: enneagram-personality.com Population Distribution
sources.methodology: Self-selected online test-takers (unique-IP and anti-bot filtering; primary type required to score >6% above second-highest).
sources.notes: Only public source verified with a complete 9-type table, sample size, date, and methodology on a single URL. Self-selected online sample.

sources.name: Truity Personality Test Statistics
sources.methodology: Self-selected Truity online test-takers (free opt-in test).
sources.date_range: Published October 2022; relationship survey at n=88,000 dated January 2021
sources.notes: Truity has never published a consolidated 9-type table; only Types 5, 7, 8, 9 are given explicit percentages. Type 7 is reported as rarest at 9%, contradicting enneagram-personality.com (13.6%) — the sharpest disagreement in the public data.

credibility_references.name: Wagner (1981)
credibility_references.citation: Wagner, J. P. (1981). A descriptive, reliability, and validity study of the Enneagram personality typology. Loyola University of Chicago.
credibility_references.contribution: Original Wagner Enneagram Personality Inventory (WEPI); foundational Enneagram–Millon correlation.

credibility_references.name: Newgent et al. (2004)
credibility_references.citation: Newgent, R. A., Parr, P. E., Newman, I., & Higgins, K. K. (2004). The Riso–Hudson Enneagram Type Indicator: Estimates of reliability and validity. Measurement and Evaluation in Counseling and Development, 36(4), 226–237.

credibility_references.name: Sutton (2007 / 2013)
credibility_references.citation: Sutton, A. (2013). But is it real? A review of research on the Enneagram. IEA Nine Points Journal.
credibility_references.contribution: Big Five correlations by Enneagram type; 75% classification accuracy.

credibility_references.name: Bartram & Brown (2005)
credibility_references.citation: Bartram, D., & Brown, A. (2005). Putting the person into personality: Enneagram and OPQ32 correlations (SHL white paper).
credibility_references.contribution: Significant Enneagram–OPQ32 correlations across multiple scales.

credibility_references.name: Hook et al. (2021)
credibility_references.citation: Hook, J. N., Hall, T. W., Davis, D. E., Van Tongeren, D. R., & Conner, M. (2021). The Enneagram: A systematic review of the literature and directions for future research. Journal of Clinical Psychology, 77(4), 865–883.
credibility_references.contribution: Systematic review — mixed but improving evidence for Enneagram construct validity.

credibility_references.name: Integrative9 iEQ9 Validity Study (2019)
credibility_references.citation: Integrative Enneagram Solutions (2019). Testing the iEQ9: Reliability and validity of the Integrative Enneagram Questionnaire.
credibility_references.contribution: Adaptive-assessment validity study; over 350,000 cumulative administrations. Per-type distribution not publicly disclosed.
